macro_segments.title: The Four-and-Five Penalty on Yom Kippur: Does Kimlei Override Payment?

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah's Case: Stealing and Slaughtering on Yom Kippur
Why Doesn't Kimlei Apply? The Problem of Lashes and Payment
Rav Ashi's Challenge: If Rabbi Meir Applies to Lashes, What About Capital Offenses?
Distinguishing Capital Offenses from Lashes-Only Violations

macro_segments.title: The Braita of Stealing and Slaughtering on Shabbat, for Idolatry, or a Condemned Ox: Three Cases of Apparent Liability Despite Disqualifying Acts

micro_segments.title: Presenting the Braita: Rabbi Meir's Position that Four-and-Five Apply in All Three Scenarios
Bracketing the Braita: Clarifying That It Discusses Slaughtering Through a Third Party
Rava's Solution: The Torah Permits Slaughtering Through an Agent Just as Selling Through an Agent
Mar Zutra's Question: If You'd Be Exempt Had You Slaughtered Personally, How Can an Agent Make You Liable?
Rav Ashi's Answer: The Fundamental Liability for Slaughtering and Selling Remains; Other Circumstances Block Payment

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Braita's Three Cases via Shechitah She'eina Re'uya (Slaughtering That Does Not Render the Meat Fit)

micro_segments.title: Moving Beyond Kimlei: Focusing on Whether Slaughtering Makes the Meat Permissible
The Case of Idolatry: When Does the Animal Become Forbidden? At the First Nick or at Completion?
Rava's Answer: The Act of Worship Occurs Only Upon Completion, But the Animal Is Forbidden from the First Nick
The Case of a Condemned Ox: How Can It Have Value If It's Prohibited in Benefit?
Rava's Multi-Part Solution: The Depositary Can Return It; It's Worth Money to the Depositary; Rabbi Meir Holds Like Rabbi Shimon on 'Something Causing Financial Loss'
Rabbi Shimon's Principle: Something That Would Cause Financial Loss Is Treated as Property with Full Monetary Value
Tosafot's Question: Why Invoke Rabbi Shimon When Rabbi Meir's Own Position on Garmi (Causation) Might Suffice?
Distinguishing Garmi (Causation Damages) from Davar Ha-Goreim (Ownership-Based Valuation)

macro_segments.title: Stealing from Father, Then Slaughtering: The Effect of Inheritance on Liability for Four-and-Five

micro_segments.title: The Question: If Father Dies After Slaughtering, Do You Pay Full Four-and-Five Despite Now Owning Part of the Animal?
The Mishnah: If Father Dies After Slaughtering, Full Liability; If Before, No Liability
First Attempt: The Distinction Is That if the Court Imposed Judgment Before the Death, the Obligation Is Fixed
Objection: The Mishnah Places the Distinction at the Timing of Slaughtering, Not Court Judgment
Revised Answer: You Can Pay Partial Four-and-Five (Only to Siblings, Not to Yourself) and Still Discharge the Obligation
Rava's Concession: He Initially Resisted But Reconsidered (the 'Meat' Pun about Fasting During the Nine Days)
Final Resolution: The Distinction Is Liability vs. Payment—Liability Must Attach to a Fully Non-Owned Animal

Modeh Ba-Knas Patur (Admitting to a Penalty Exempts Payment)
Father's Death Before/After Slaughtering—Effect on Liability
Court Judgment (Amad Ba-Din) and Fixed Obligations
